title: Thermodynamic and structural behavior of one-dimensional divalent patchy hard rods: Wertheim's first-order thermodynamic perturbation theory versus exact results
authors: Ana M. Montero, Andrés Santos, Péter Gurin, Szabolcs Varga
abstract: We investigate the thermodynamic and structural properties of divalent patchy hard rods confined to a one-dimensional channel by modeling the bonding sites as attractive square-well (SW) patches located at the rod tips. The zero-range sticky limit is recovered by letting the well width vanish while keeping the stickiness parameter finite. While Wertheim's first-order thermodynamic perturbation theory (TPT1) becomes exact in this sticky limit, it fails for finite-range site-site interactions. We show that the theory can be made exact in one dimension by replacing the standard law of mass action with an exact relation between the density and the fraction of unbonded sites, together with an exact bonding free-energy contribution. Finite-range SW sites produce a richer structural behavior than sticky sites, including monotonic and oscillatory asymptotic decay of the pair correlation function, separated by the Fisher--Widom line. In the monotonic regime, the correlation length exhibits an absolute maximum defining the Widom line, while in the oscillatory regime it may display a local maximum and minimum, whose locus defines the ``Extrema of the Correlation length under Oscillatory decay'' (ECO) line. These features disappear in the sticky limit, where the system remains entirely in the oscillatory regime. We also show that the high-pressure behavior of the correlation length changes from $ξ\sim p^2$ for finite-range SW sites to $ξ\sim p^3$ in the sticky limit.
summary: This paper provides an exact analytical treatment of 1D patchy hard rods to demonstrate where standard association theories fail. It reveals that finite-range interactions introduce complex structural transitions, such as the ECO line, which are absent in the simpler sticky-limit model.
main_problem: The study investigates the accuracy of Wertheim's first-order thermodynamic perturbation theory (TPT1) for 1D divalent patchy hard rods and explores the structural complexity arising from finite-range interactions.
main_result: The authors show that while TPT1 is exact in the zero-range sticky limit, it fails for finite-range interactions; they identify new structural features like the ECO line and demonstrate that the high-pressure scaling of the correlation length changes depending on the interaction range.
method: The authors use analytical derivations via the isothermal-isobaric ensemble and Laplace-pole analysis of the structure factor to compare exact solutions against TPT1 approximations.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional system of divalent patchy hard rods with a hard-core diameter sigma and attractive square-well patches of width delta and depth epsilon located at the rod tips.
key_observables: Pressure, fraction of unbonded sites, pair correlation function, correlation length, and structural lines (Fisher-Widom, Widom, and ECO lines).
important_parameters: Rod length (sigma), interaction range (delta), well depth (epsilon), inverse stickiness (tau), and number density (rho).
main_assumptions: The system is confined to a strictly 1D channel (single-file regime) preventing rotation or particle overtaking; the study focuses on the nearest-neighbor bonding limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the 1D rods; Figure 2 compares exact vs TPT1 pressure; Figure 3 shows the density dependence of the pressure ratio; Figure 5 illustrates structural lines and correlation length behavior.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the 1D patchy model, derives exact thermodynamic and structural properties using Laplace transforms, compares these results to Wertheim's TPT1, and analyzes various structural regimes and asymptotic limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of divalent patchy hard rods of length σ aligned along the x-axis. Particles cannot overlap (i.e., x > σ), but neighboring rods can form bonds when σ < x < σ + δ through the interaction between the right (R) site of the left particle and the left (L) site of the right particle. A monomer and a dimer are shown for illustration.
FIG. 2. Density dependence of the ratios (a) p/pTPT1 and (b) X/XTPT1 for divalent patchy hard rods with δ∗= 0.1 and τ = 100, 0.02, and 10−3.
FIG. 4. Fraction of free monomers (⟨X0⟩), terminal rods (⟨X1⟩), and internal rods (⟨X2⟩) as functions of density for τ = 0.1 and δ∗= 1, 0.5, 0.1, and 0 (sticky limit).

FIG. 3. Density dependence of the pressure ratio p/pT for di- valent patchy hard rods with (a) τ = 100, (b) τ = 0.02, and (c) τ = 10−3. In each panel, the attractive ranges are δ∗= 1, 0.5, 0.1, and 0 (sticky limit).

FIG. 5. Structural diagram in the τ–ρ∗plane for divalent patchy hard rods with δ∗= 0.5. The FW line (solid curve) separates the regions of monotonic and oscillatory asymptotic decay of the pair correlation function. Below the FW line, the Widom line (dash-dotted curve) is deﬁned as the locus of ab- solute maxima of the correlation length ξ in the monotonic regime. In the oscillatory regime, ξ may exhibit a weak lo- cal maximum followed by a local minimum; the locus of these extrema deﬁnes the ECO line (dashed curve). The horizontal dotted lines correspond to τ = 0.2, 0.02, 3 × 10−3, and 10−4. Circles, upward triangles, and downward triangles indicate the intersections with the FW, Widom,...
